headline: Jeannette Rankin Becomes First Woman Elected to U.S. Congress, 1916
body:
Now, some folks might tell you that November 7, 1916, was just another Tuesday, but if you believe that, you probably also believe in honest politicians. No, sir, that day, out in the wild and woolly West, in a place called Montana, something happened that made a good many jaws drop, and a few more hats fly into the air. A woman, by the name of Jeannette Rankin, got herself elected to the United States Congress. Yes, you heard that right. A woman. Before half the country’s women even had the right to cast a ballot, mind you. It was a peculiar sort of progress, like building the roof before the foundation, but progress it was, nonetheless. 

Miss Rankin, she wasn't born yesterday, nor was she born in some fancy parlor with a silver spoon in her mouth. She hailed from a ranch near Missoula, a place where folks learned early on that if you wanted something done, you generally had to do it yourself. Her parents, bless their forward-thinking souls, encouraged her to look beyond the kitchen and the sewing basket, a notion that was still considered downright radical in many parts of this great republic. She went to the University of Montana, then off to the New York School of Philanthropy, which, I reckon, taught her a thing or two about the peculiar habits of mankind, both rich and poor. She even spent a spell as a social worker, which is a profession that requires the patience of Job and the hide of a rhinoceros, if you ask me.

But her true calling, it seemed, was to stir up the political pot. She’d been a tireless advocate for women’s suffrage, a cause that, in Montana, had already seen some success. The men of the Treasure State, perhaps softened by the vastness of the landscape or the sheer grit of their womenfolk, had granted women the vote back in 1914. This was a good six years before the whole nation finally caught up with the idea, proving once again that the West, for all its rough edges, sometimes had a clearer view of common sense than the more ‘civilized’ East. Miss Rankin, a Republican, mind you, decided to put this newfangled equality to the test. She ran for one of Montana’s two at-large congressional seats, and by golly, she won. The election certificate, signed by the governor and secretary of state, made it official. The news, I imagine, caused a good many old boys in smoke-filled rooms to choke on their cigars.

When she arrived in Washington, D.C., the following year, the whole country was watching, wondering if a woman could truly handle the rough-and-tumble world of national politics. Some probably expected her to faint at the first sign of a heated debate, or perhaps demand a separate ladies’ entrance to the Capitol. But Jeannette Rankin, she had other ideas. Her very first vote, a matter of war and peace, was a declaration in itself. The nation was itching for a fight, eager to plunge into the Great War brewing across the ocean. But Miss Rankin, a dedicated pacifist, stood firm. She cast her vote against entry into World War I. Now, a good many men, fifty-five of them to be precise, voted the same way, but it was Rankin’s vote that drew the most fire, and the most ink. Some declared it proof that women were too emotional for leadership, while others hailed her courage. It just goes to show, a woman doing the same thing as a man can still cause twice the fuss.

She continued to champion the cause of women’s voting rights, even serving on the Committee on Woman Suffrage, pushing for a federal amendment. The House, in its infinite wisdom, passed it in 1918, but the Senate, being the Senate, let it die a quiet death. It wasn\'t until 1920, after her single term was done, that the 19th Amendment finally became law, granting all American women the right to vote. It was a long road, paved with stubbornness and shoe leather, and Miss Rankin had worn out a good many pairs herself. She once remarked, with that dry wit that often accompanies a lifetime of fighting uphill battles, “I worked for suffrage for years, and got it. I’ve worked for peace for 55 years and haven’t come close.” A simple statement, that, but one that speaks volumes about the nature of human endeavor, and the enduring struggle for a better world. And so, the first woman in Congress, a Montanan through and through, left her mark, not with a bang, but with a quiet, resolute vote against the folly of war, and a lifetime dedicated to the notion that common sense, like a good river, will eventually find its way to the sea.
